ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'toast (food)' class. No specific multigrain butter sugar toast entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and similar products — toast bread
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers toast bread as a category. No multigrain butter sugar toast specific code exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226559009 is the closest available concept for toast as a food product. No multigrain-specific code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 2 slices (about 90 g) of multigrain toast spread with butter and sugar, as typically served at a Singapore kopitiam or Toast Box outlet.

nutrient_source.title: Bread, whole-wheat, commercially prepared (USDA SR Legacy 18069) and Butter, salted (USDA SR Legacy 01001), adapted for multigrain butter sugar toast composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry for multigrain butter sugar toast was found. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for multigrain/whole-wheat bread (FDC 325871), salted butter (FDC 173410), and white sugar (FDC 169655), combined in proportions consistent with a typical kopitiam butter sugar toast preparation: approximately 70 g multigrain bread, 12 g butter, 8 g sugar per 90 g serving. Per-100 g values are derived from this weighted composite.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; multigrain bread GI reference (GI ~65, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore kopitiam multigrain butter sugar toast. GI of 65 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 International GI Tables for multigrain bread (glucose reference scale). The addition of butter (fat) and sugar (sucrose, GI ~65) does not substantially alter the composite GI at typical serving proportions. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (65 × 44.9) / 100 ≈ 29.2 for a 90 g serving (44.9 g available carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from salted butter and bread
hypertension.guidance: A 90 g serving provides approximately 279 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit intake and request unsalted butter where available, as regular consumption may contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load and added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 29 per serving and 11.5 g of added sugar per serving, this toast is unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion reduction, substitution with a lower-GI bread, and omission of added sugar are recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and trans fat from butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving contributes approximately 5.2 g saturated fat and 0.2 g trans fat from butter, which may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider plant-based spread alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Multigrain Butter Sugar Toast is a popular Singaporean kopitiam breakfast item consisting of freshly toasted slices of multigrain bread generously spread with cold salted butter and sprinkled or layered with white sugar. It is a staple offering at chains such as Toast Box and Ya Kun Kaya Toast, typically served alongside soft-boiled eggs and a cup of kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea). The multigrain bread provides a slightly nutty, hearty flavour and a firmer texture compared to white bread, while the cold butter melts partially on the warm toast, creating a rich, creamy contrast. The added sugar contributes sweetness and a light crunch. Nutritionally, a standard two-slice serving (approximately 90 g) delivers around 286 kcal, 6.1 g protein, 9.2 g fat (of which 5.2 g is saturated), and 44.6 g carbohydrate including 11.5 g sugar. The glycemic load is high at approximately 29 per serving, making this a food of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The sodium content (~279 mg per serving) warrants attention for those with hypertension. The multigrain base contributes modest dietary fibre (~2.8 g per serving), which is marginally beneficial compared to white bread toast. This dish is vegetarian and generally halal-certified at major kopitiam chains in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in multigrain butter sugar toast?
Is multigrain toast from Toast Box healthy?
What is the GI of multigrain bread toast?
Can diabetics eat butter sugar toast?

Is Toast Box multigrain toast halal?
What nutrients are in Singapore kopi shop toast?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry for multigrain butter sugar toast was identified. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for multigrain bread, salted butter, and white sugar in proportions consistent with a standard kopitiam serving. GI is analogously estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for multigrain bread. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in USDA SR Legacy and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy entries for multigrain/whole-wheat bread (FDC 325871), salted butter (FDC 173410), and white sugar (FDC 169655) in a ratio of approximately 70:12:8 by weight per 90 g serving, then normalised to per-100 g. 2. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; this is confirmed zero as multigrain bread, butter, and sugar contain negligible vitamin C (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for all three components). 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for multigrain bread; no direct GI measurement for this specific kopitiam preparation exists. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.7), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9), trans_fat_g (0.2), potassium_mg (145.0), calcium_mg (52.0), iron_mg (1.8), cholesterol_mg (26.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (62.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.18), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.12), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (2.1), vitamin_b6_mg (0.08), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.04), folate_mcg_dfe (28.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.1), vitamin_e_mg (0.5), phosphorus_mg (110.0), magnesium_mg (28.0), zinc_mg (0.8), selenium_mcg (14.0), water_g (30.5) are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite; no Singapore-specific micronutrient data available. 5. Halal status reflects general practice at major kopitiam chains in Singapore; individual outlet certification should be verified. 6. Price data from field survey data (SGD 3.90) is preserved from the input record for reference only and is not used in nutritional calculations. 7. The food_name has been normalised from 'Multigrain Traditional Butter Sugar Toast' to 'Multigrain Butter Sugar Toast' as the canonical English name; the original menu name is preserved in aliases.

Constructed composite nutrient profile from USDA SR Legacy for multigrain bread, salted butter, and white sugar in kopitiam-typical proportions.
Added all mandatory Singaporean local-language names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Sourced GI of 65 from University of Sydney GI Database / 2021 International GI Tables for multigrain bread as analogous estimate.
Documented vitamin_c_mg = 0.0 as confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for all component foods.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all language-specific alias strings appear verbatim in flat aliases.
Set all ontology match_type values to allowed strings only (closest_parent); added gap notes for all three ontology systems.
Documented all estimated micronutrient fields in notes with source reference.
